revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:大東神社]]
{{Infobox religious building| | Name = Daitō Jinja (大東神社) | image = [[File:Daito-jinja, Ome.jpg|250px]] | Location = {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}} {{ill|Ōme, Tokyo|en|Ōme, Tokyo|qid=Q237683|lt=Ōme, Tokyo|1=Ōme, Tokyo}} {{ill|Imai (Ome City)|ja|今井 (青梅市)|lt=Imai|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Imai (Ome City)|1=Imai (Ome City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q30927152}} | Latitude Degrees = 35 | Latitude Minutes = 47 | Latitude Seconds = 43 | Longitude Degrees = 139 | Longitude Minutes = 19 | Longitude Seconds = 27 | Enshrined Deity = {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} | Divine Object = | Shrine Rank = | Founded = [[1979]] | Main Hall = | Alternate Name = | Place Marker = | Annual Festival = November 3 | Shinto Rituals = | Map = Japan Tokyo }} '''Daitō Jinja''' (大東神社【だいとうじんじゃ】) is a Shinto shrine located in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo Metropolis|1=Tokyo}} {{ill|Ōme, Tokyo|en|Ōme, Tokyo|qid=Q237683|lt=Ōme, Tokyo|1=Ōme, Tokyo}}. The enshrined deities include, beginning with {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}}, as well as the myriad gods of the heavenly realm, the spirits of all fallen comrades, and the ancestral spirits of individual families. == History == == Overview == In Showa 14, Masaharu Kageyama, the founder of {{ill|Daitō Juku|ja|大東塾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitō Juku|1=Daitō Juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10856370}}, who actively participated in the Showa Restoration Movement, initiated the establishment of a “village of eternal loyalty to the emperor.” After enduring immense hardships in settlement and reclamation, he founded the “Daitō Farm” on the land of Imai in Ome City. On April 1, Showa 54, after completing projects such as the central joint tombstone for the fourteen martyrs of Daitō Juku, the monument for the enshrinement of all fallen comrades, the Daitō Cemetery, and a divine offering field, the long-awaited grand enshrinement festival for “Daitō Jinja” was solemnly held. Daitō Jinja was constructed using materials bestowed by {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|qid=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}} and {{ill|Meiji Shrine|en|Meiji Shrine|qid=Q287165|lt=Meiji Jingu|1=Meiji Shrine}}, and directly beneath the floor of the main hall, precious stones donated by the juku head and senior officials from major shrines nationwide prior to the war have been enshrined as talismans. Within the central main hall, {{ill|Amaterasu Omikami|ja|天照大御神|1=Amaterasu|lt=Amaterasu Ōmikami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Amaterasu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 天照大神}} and the myriad gods of the heavenly realm are enshrined; in the right “Seirei-den” the spirits of all fallen comrades are honored, while in the left “Sorei-den” the ancestral spirits of each family are venerated. On May 25, shortly after the enshrinement of Daitō Jinja, Juku Head {{ill|Masaharu Kageyama|ja|影山正治|lt=Masaharu Kageyama|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masaharu Kageyama|1=Masaharu Kageyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488103}} committed ritual suicide by disembowelment atop a hill behind the monument for the fourteen martyrs. His farewell note and death poem read as follows: 身一つをみづ玉串とささげまつり 御世を祈らむみたまらとともに 民族の本ついのちのふるさとへはやはやかへれ 戦後日本よ ("I offer my very self as a tamagushi, praying for our world; Hasten the return of our people's very life to its ancestral home—post-war Japan.") On April 2, Heisei 22, at a re-enshrinement ceremony, the total number of fallen comrades enshrined in the Seirei-den reached 624, including the spirit of the late Masaharu Kageyama. Daitō Jinja not only serves as the guardian deity for {{ill|Daitō Juku|ja|大東塾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitō Juku|1=Daitō Juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10856370}} and the unified body of the Fuji Kadōkai, but also functions as the national Daitō Jinja—a shrine established for the protection of imperial descendants and the manifestation of a divine Japan based on Shimmei—and stands as an eternal stronghold for the protection of the heavenly realm. === Shrine Grounds === Within the shrine grounds there are various tombstones, cemeteries, song monuments, and statues. These include the central joint tombstone for the fourteen martyrs, the monument and song monument commemorating the late Masaharu Kageyama, statues of Daikuson and Shōkuson, the Japan–Korea Unification Memorial Tower, and the Daitō Cemetery. === Detached Enclosures === Detached enclosures centered on the fourteen martyrs' joint tombstone and the song monument of Juku Head Kageyama exist in {{ill|Iwate, Iwate Town|ja|岩手県岩手町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate, Iwate Town|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Yamabu City, Chiba|ja|千葉県山武市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamabu City, Chiba|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Kurashiki City, Okayama|ja|岡山県倉敷市|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurashiki City, Okayama|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. === Daitō Jinja Support Association === Daitō Jinja has a Support Association whose main activities are as follows: * Gathering and nurturing an organization of devotees of Daitō Jinja. * Sponsoring the shrine’s festivals and ceremonial events. * Supporting projects related to the maintenance and upkeep of the shrine’s facilities and precincts. * Undertaking other activities necessary to achieve the Association’s objectives. === Daitō Jinja Labor Service === At Daitō Jinja, labor service programs are conducted. With the aim of nurturing true Japanese spirit, the shrine also accepts training and discipline programs for youth and students. Participants reside at the juku or dojo for periods ranging from day trips to several days, engaging in studies of kokugaku and waka poetry, martial arts training, as well as agricultural work and grass cutting. == References == * “Funi” * “Michi no Tomo” * “Daitō Jinja Pamphlet” == Related Articles == * {{ill|Daitō Juku|ja|大東塾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daitō Juku|1=Daitō Juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10856370}} * {{ill|Masaharu Kageyama|ja|影山正治|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Masaharu Kageyama|1=Masaharu Kageyama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488103}} {{Shinto Side|qq=}} {{Normdaten|qq=}} {{shinto-stub|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|大東神社 (青梅市)|version=103523133|comment=Imported full ja history}}
